coredata.dc:title: A numerical approach to damage modelling for mechanical assessment of structural batteries
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Weina
Ma, Dayou
Manes, Andrea
coredata.dc:description: Integrating lithium-ion polymer (LiPo) batteries into composite structures offers the potential to create multifunctional structural batteries that enhance both energy efficiency and structural reinforcement for lightweight design. While current research on structural batteries predominantly relies on experimental methods, there is a need for more systematic numerical studies to assess their structural integrity. This paper presents a holistic finite element (FE) computational model to evaluate the structural batteries’ mechanical performance and damage mechanisms under complex load cases. Various damage criteria and interactive models were employed to examine the behaviour of composite laminates and sandwich composites incorporating LiPo cells. Comparative analysis with experimental data from literature confirmed the FE model’s applicability and generality, though it showed slight variations in predictive modulus and post-yielding behaviour under bending. The numerical results indicate that the structural performance depends on specific design configurations and loading types. Sandwich composites exhibited minimal impacts resulting from battery integration, while the composite laminates had a notable decrease in structural strength. In addition, the presence of batteries strongly influenced the loading response and damage modes of the host structures. This numerical work provides valuable insights into the future design and health monitoring of structural energy storage systems.

head.ce:title: A numerical approach to damage modelling for mechanical assessment of structural batteries

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Politecnico di Milano, Department of Mechanical Engineering, via la Masa, 1, 20156 Milan, Italy
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Politecnico di Milano, Department of Mechanical Engineering, via la Masa, 1, 20156 Milan, Italy

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: via La Mass, 1, Milan 20156, Italy.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Integrating lithium-ion polymer (LiPo) batteries into composite structures offers the potential to create multifunctional structural batteries that enhance both energy efficiency and structural reinforcement for lightweight design. While current research on structural batteries predominantly relies on experimental methods, there is a need for more systematic numerical studies to assess their structural integrity. This paper presents a holistic finite element (FE) computational model to evaluate the structural batteries’ mechanical performance and damage mechanisms under complex load cases. Various damage criteria and interactive models were employed to examine the behaviour of composite laminates and sandwich composites incorporating LiPo cells. Comparative analysis with experimental data from literature confirmed the FE model’s applicability and generality, though it showed slight variations in predictive modulus and post-yielding behaviour under bending. The numerical results indicate that the structural performance depends on specific design configurations and loading types. Sandwich composites exhibited minimal impacts resulting from battery integration, while the composite laminates had a notable decrease in structural strength. In addition, the presence of batteries strongly influenced the loading response and damage modes of the host structures. This numerical work provides valuable insights into the future design and health monitoring of structural energy storage systems.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Growing concerns over traffic pollution and fossil fuel consumption have spurred interest in electric vehicles (EVs) due to their lower greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions, reduced fuel usage, and economic advantages. An innovative structural batteries, which integrates energy storage systems with load-bearing elements, becomes a strong candidate for the power devices of EVs. This novel concept transforms energy systems from mere addendums to integral parts of the vehicle structure, potentially decreasing the vehicle’s weight and improving the spatial design of EVs. Early research on structural batteries was conducted by Thomas and Qidwai [1], who demonstrated the feasibility of extending the range of unmanned aerial vehicles (UAVs) by incorporating lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) into the wings. Follow-up research verified that implementing this idea to car roofs could decrease the vehicle’s weight by around 20 % [2], resulting in a 70 % increase in mileage [3]. Although structural batteries are still in the early stages compared to conventional lithium-ion batteries [4], with large-scale manufacturing and widespread application in electric vehicles remaining long-term goals, they have already shown considerable potential in portable electronics [5], toy vehicles [6], CubeSats [7], and biomimetic robots [8]. Recent studies have also confirmed their impressive manufacturability and ability to achieve notable mechanical and electrical performance simultaneously [9–12].

Two main approaches exist for creating structural energy storage systems [5,13–16]: coupled and decoupled structural batteries. Coupled systems typically use multifunctional materials like carbon fibre as both energy storage and structural components to develop structural battery composites (SBCs). However, replacing traditional battery electrodes with carbon fibre often reduces ionic conductivity and increases electrical resistance, leading to inferior energy density and mechanical performance of SBCs [7,13,17,18]. The decoupled structural battery is a unified structure that combines an energy storage system with other structural elements, such as integrating LIBs into composite laminates. These structures can be in the shape of sandwiches, consisting of a skin that bears the load (carbon fibre, glass fibre, aluminium, or polylactic acid) and a thick, lightweight core (foam or carbon fibre) [19–22]. Alternatively, they can be laminated structural batteries (LSBs) [23]. Decoupled structural batteries can maintain the original performance of each component and offer greater design flexibility due to their manufacturing features.

A large amount of research effort has been put into understanding how multifunctional structural batteries will react mechanically during operation. Research by Pereira et al. [20] demonstrated a small improvement in the tensile strength when thin-film lithium energy cells were embedded into composite materials. This observation was also discovered by Shalouf et al. [24], where the average failure stress of sandwich structural batteries under tension and bending was slightly higher to that of uni-functional sandwich composites. However, their study showed a significant reduction (50 %) in the ultimate compressive stress. The compressive strength degradation phenomenon was further corroborated by Wang et al. [25], who documented an 8.6 % decrease in the compressive strength of composites integrated with LIBs. Their research also examined the flexural performance of structural batteries, finding a 19.5 % decline in flexural stiffness when the battery was bonded with carbon fibre-reinforced polymer (CFRP) and foam core, and a decrease of 45 % for structures without adhesive. These results contrast with those of Thomas and Qidwai [26], who initially observed increased bending stiffness in multifunctional structure-battery systems, but later noted that flexural strength decreased due to delamination of the adhesive layer. To address these issues, Ladpli et al. [27] suggested using a rivet topology to mitigate shear movement between cell layers and alleviate excessive deformation, thereby improving the flexural rigidity of multifunctional energy storage composites. For impact testing, Hu et al. [28] reported that integrating lithium-ion batteries into composite laminates could effectively enhance the energy absorption capacity of the original structure, with the inclusion of a foam core layer further increasing the energy absorption density. However, as the impact energy increased, Li et al. [29] observed that the capacity of the embedded batteries gradually declined, primarily due to cracks and exfoliation in the active layers caused by the impact load. All these related investigations may present the complexity of structural battery’s mechanical behaviours.

The preceding discussion demonstrates the case-by-case dependency of mechanical properties and the impact of embedded batteries on structural behaviour, with most research relying on experimental data. The mechanical characteristics of the multifunctional energy storage system can be influenced by several factors, including configurations, material types, interfacial properties, adhesive properties, quantities of batteries, and loading conditions [30]. However, there has yet to be a standardised approach for designing and manufacturing multifunctional structural energy devices [31]. Furthermore, limited attention has been paid to numerical simulations for the performance assessment, which could facilitate optimal configuration design and safety management of structural energy storage systems. Several studies covered numerical modelling, but the majority have concentrated on coupled structural batteries and their mechanical–electrical coupling behaviour [32–37]. The modelling strategies for decoupled-type structural batteries, particularly their mechanical response and failure to external loads, remain underexplored and necessitate further investigation. Besides, limited work has been done on general material models, damage modelling, and interface/connection properties for structural batteries [22,38–41]. Numerical modelling is essential as it can complement physical testing, thereby reducing costs and offering potential insights for structural standardization.

To contribute to the field of numerical modelling for multifunctional structural batteries, this study introduces a high-fidelity numerical methodology for decoupled structural batteries. The goal is to understand the effect of embedded battery on the mechanical performance of host structures and to investigate the interplay of damage mechanisms among each component under simple loadings, such as tension, compression, and three-point bending, as well as more complex loading conditions like low-velocity impact. Besides loading conditions, two multifunctional energy storage device configurations will be employed: sandwich composite and composite laminate with embedded lithium-ion polymer (LiPo) batteries. These configurations consist of CFRP laminates, polyvinyl chloride (PVC) foam and LiPo batteries. By validating the numerical model against experimental results, a holistic numerical model will be established across a range of loading conditions and configurations. This will contribute to safety analysis and optimization of decoupled structural batteries, while also providing design implications for other innovative integrated composites. The paper is organized as follows: Section 2 provides an overview of the modelling methodologies used for each constituent of the multifunctional structures, along with their respective failure mechanisms. Section 3 presents detailed material parameters and finite element setups for numerical testing. Section 4 offers an in-depth discussion of the model’s precision and the mechanical behaviour of each specimen when subjected to external forces. Section 5 concludes the key findings of this study and outlines future research directions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Polyvinyl chloride foam and crushable foam model
ce:section.ce:para: The closed-cell PVC foam, known for its low density and high energy absorption, is widely used as core material in sandwich composite structures. The hardening behaviour of PVC foam under compression differs from what is observed in the tensile and shear tests. Instead of undergoing strain hardening after the initial linear elastic phase, the PVC foam experiences a plateau and subsequent densification zone during compression [44]. This plateau results from the collapse of cellular structures within the foam. Simulating such phenomenon in software poses difficulties due to its complex nature, which is heavily influenced by foam density and loading rate. Additionally, the presence of gases within closed-cell foam further increases modelling difficulty [45]. Various methods have been developed and tested to predict the crushing behaviour of cellular foam, including Deshpande and Fleck model [46], Von-Mises yield criterion [47], and Crushable foam model [48]. The current study employs the crushable foam model to simulate the compressive characteristics and deterioration of PVC foam during the bending test.

Two widely used material models for foam modelling are crushable foam models with volumetric hardening and isotropic hardening. The latter was initially created by Deshpande and Fleck [49] for metallic foams, which differs from our case. Therefore, only the model with volumetric hardening will be applied.

As proposed by Zhu et al. [50], the modelling of LiPo batteries usually consists of three classes: micro/meso-level, macro-level, and macro-system level. The first two pertain to the material and cell levels, while the latter relates to the entirety of the battery pack or module in EVs. At the micro/meso level, a LiPo cell is a multi-layered structure composed of a cathode, anode, and separators. These components are submerged in electrolytes, and a pouch or shell encloses the entire structure. Modelling such structure requires an in-depth understanding of the mechanical properties, constitutive models, and damage mechanisms for each element above, which is tedious and will result in increased computational efforts. Moreover, even for the same type of battery, the inside materials and dimensions differ among battery manufacturers, and the test on the ingredients is challenging to implement due to their limited size [50].

Regarding marco-level modelling, there are two ways included [50]: 1) Representative Volume Elements (RVE) approach: RVE is the smallest volume that can be measured to represent the properties of the entire structure. The battery case of the RVE is composed of active layers, graphite layers, foils, and separators [51]. 2) Homogenized model: the most straightforward approach assumes that the lithium battery is a uniform material, and the modelling only requires an appropriate constitutive equation for the structure. Some battery characteristics can be considered before choosing the constitutive model and the setting of parameters [50]: densification, pressure dependence, anisotropy, and shear band formation. Since the current study concentrates more on the global mechanical response of multifunctional energy storage devices from a macroscopic view instead of their microscopic changes, at the meanwhile, considering the computational consume, the homogenized approach will be adopted and is apparently sufficient to understand the elasticity, plasticity, as well as the degradation of the LiPo battery and will be adopted to meet the research needs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Interfacial delamination and cohesive zone modelling
ce:section.ce:para:
The multifunctional energy storage system studied in this paper inherently takes a sandwich form. For the healthy evaluation of the sandwich composite structure used for automotive applications [52], the interfacial delamination should be assessed and correctly modelled because it will drastically reduce the load-carrying capacity of the entire structure and affect dynamic behaviour [53]. The adhesive and interfacial debonding of contact pairs can be defined using a cohesive zone model (CZM) conjugated with normal surface contact properties in ABAQUS [54]. The CZM is proposed by Anderson [55] and is based on the traction-separation law, as shown

Uniaxial tensile and three-point bending tests have been carried out on sandwich composite structures, while compressive and low-velocity impact tests have been considered for composite laminate structures. To create the multifunctional energy storage system, cut-offs matching the dimensions of LiPo battery were made within the core of each structure to house the cell. The primary components of the multifunctional energy storage device included CFRP, commercial H100 PVC foam, and LiPo battery [21,40,41,57].

The CFRP used for numerical analysis comprised plain-woven unidirectional T300 carbon fabric arranged in a lay-up sequence of [0/90]s and epoxy resin West System 105/206. Each ply of the laminate had a thickness of 0.25 mm, and detailed material properties for the composite laminate are listed in Table 1 . The composite laminate was modelled as a hexahedral continuum shell with reduced integration (SC8R) in ABAQUS/Explicit to accurately represent the orthotropic response of CFRP. Hashin initiation criterion combined with energy-based damage evolution was adopted for the degradation assessment of CFRP laminates.

The core material for the lightweight sandwich energy storage system was close-cell PVC foam (Divinycell H100) with a 100 kg/m3 density. The plasticity of the foam core in the tensile case was characterized by the loading curve from [60], with the ductile damage model assigned for failure analysis. In three-point bending, cellular collapse and indentation are evident; thereby, a crushable foam model with data from [61] conjugated with a compressive hardening curve [62] was applied. Due to the high susceptibility of foam to shear failure under bending conditions, a shear failure model combined with displacement-based damage evolution was employed. To ensure geometrical compatibility, the foam was meshed using a C3D8R type element, an 8-node linear brick with reduced integration. Input parameters for the foam in numerical simulations are presented in Table 2 .

The LiPo battery was 40 × 30 × 4 mm in size, with relative mechanical and electrical properties shown in Table 3 . The battery was modelled as a homogenized material, with its plasticity determined by hardening curves obtained from uniaxial tensile tests [63] and compression tests [41]. The LiPo cell was assumed to be prone to tension under impact and three-point bending conditions, therefore, the tensile hardening curve was applied for these two scenarios. The battery meshing employed the same element type as the foam (C3D8R). A ductile damage model was adopted for damage assessment, with relevant data sourced from [63,64]. Element deletion was activated for all materials to monitor failure status.

The finite element analysis (FEA) of uniaxial tensile, compressive, three-point bending, and low-velocity impact tests on multifunctional energy storage structures were executed using ABAQUS/Explicit. A ratio below 5 % between the kinetic and internal energy of the specimens during the job’s execution was tracked and kept to ensure a quasi-static loading condition and preserve result accuracy. Mesh size for the simulations was determined through a convergence study to optimize the balance between computational efficiency and numerical accuracy. A denser mesh was applied to the loading areas and the region surrounding the battery, while a coarser mesh was used for the remaining regions. Resin-rich zones from fabrication and internal wires were neglected in numerical simulations due to their minimal impact on the performance of multifunctional devices.

The sandwich composite consisted of two thin CFRP skins, each measuring 0.75 mm thick, with three layers of laminate arranged in [0/90/0] and a foam core with a thickness of 4 mm. The overall dimensions of the sandwich structure were 370 × 50 × 5.5 mm, and featured configurations with one, three and five batteries as illustrated in Fig. 1 (a). Fig. 1 (b) depicts the numerical setup for tensile test. The numerical test was established to replicate the standard tensile test for polymer matrix composite material according to ASTM D3039 [40,65]. All degrees of freedom (DOFs) were fixed at the bottom surface of the specimen, and a boundary condition of UR1 = UR2 = UR3 = 0 was applied to the top surface. A reference point was coupled with the top surface to assign an axial displacement of 6 mm with a step time of 0.01 s. Since tensile loading typically does not result in debonding within the sandwich structure, all contact surfaces were connected using Tie constraints, assuming no delamination occurs at CFRP-foam, foam-battery, and CFRP-battery interfaces. The batteries and their surrounding CFRP and foam areas were meshed with an element size of 2 mm, whereas the remaining zones were meshed with 4 mm.

The composite laminate used for the compressive test had 150 × 100 × 6 mm dimensions. The centre 16 piles of composite laminate were cut off at the same size as the battery to host it, while the remaining two sets of 4 uncut piles adhere to the upper and lower surface to surround the structure altogether. Fig. 2 (a) illustrates the specimen configurations, with boundary and loading conditions replicating the experiment [41,66]. The composite laminate was split into three parts. All the DOFs were constrained entirely in the lower section, and a boundary condition of U3 = UR1 = UR2 = UR3 = 0 was set for the top and middle parts to ensure structural stability and prevent potential buckling during compression. The gauge length was 100 mm for laminates with two vertical batteries and 120 mm for all other specimens to facilitate further strain calculation. A reference point was established, coupling with the top segment of the specimen to impose a displacement of 1.5 mm. The composite laminate was modelled using mesh elements with sizes ranging from 2 to 3 mm. A refined mesh with a 2 mm element size was specifically applied over a 40 mm long region around the cell to improve the accuracy of the simulation in this critical area. Throughout the loading process, it is assumed that all contact surfaces were perfectly bonded using the Tie contact algorithm and that there was no delamination between the contact pairs during the loading.

The sandwich sample used for the three-point bending test was 250 × 50 × 5.5 mm, with two 0.75 mm thick CFRP skins and a 4 mm thick core. Fig. 3 (a) illustrates the layouts of the sandwich composites prepared for the numerical tests, showing different quantities and arrangements of batteries. The numerical test was developed in accordance with the ASTM D790 standard [21,67]. Two bending tests were conducted with span lengths of 90 mm and 200 mm, as shown in Fig. 3 (b). The testing model comprised a sandwich structural energy storage system, a loading nose, and two supports. The loading nose and supports had the same properties as steel (E = 210 GPa, υ = 0.3) and were modelled as cylinder rollers using C3D8R elements. Since there was no need to examine the deformation and stress on the loading noses and supports, they were constrained as rigid bodies.

The interaction between the loading and support rods with the surfaces of sandwich structural batteries was determined by the built-in general contact algorithm in ABAQUS/Explicit. The contact properties were set as normal-hard and tangential-penalty, along with a frictional coefficient of 0.25, which is an approximate estimation for CFRP-steel [68]. Since sandwich structures often experience delamination under dynamic bending loading, a cohesive contact algorithm was applied to delineate the interlayer behaviour between the facing sheets, the foam core, and embedded batteries in the sandwich structure. The relevant parameters of the cohesive model are presented in Table 4 . Reference points were created in the centres of the three cylindrical rollers for the loading assignment. Two supports were fixed by “Encastre”, while a 10 mm displacement was imposed at the loading nose. The sandwich composites were meshed using eight-node hexahedral elements, with element sizes ranging from 1.5 to 3.5 mm. An area of 130 × 50 mm covering the batteries and surrounding CFRP and foam was meshed with high density using a 1.5 mm element size.

The impact testing was conducted numerically according to the references [57,71]. The testing model comprised three main elements: a composite laminate, a hemispherical impactor, and a supported base. The dimensions of the composite specimen were 150 × 100 × 5.5 mm. A cutout was made in the middle of 16 plies of carbon fabric with the same battery size to fit it. The remaining three layers of laminate were then joined to the top and bottom surfaces. The configurations of specimens and testing model are illustrated in Fig. 4 (a). The composite laminate was positioned on a 300 × 300 × 6 mm supported base, with a central cutout of 125 × 75 × 6 mm. The LiPo battery was situated beneath the centre of the semi-spherical impactor with a radius of 8.5 mm. The impactor was modelled as a discrete rigid shell element with a point mass of 2.8 kg assigned. The loading base was meshed using 3D solid, isotropic elements (C3D8R) with material properties equivalent to steel (E = 210 GPa, υ = 0.3). It is assumed that the impactor and support base do not undergo any permanent deformation; they were considered to be confined as rigid bodies. The mesh size of composite laminate and battery was 1 mm, 2 mm for the impactor, and 4 mm for the support base.

A reference point was positioned at the centre of the impactor to impose the impact loading, with 2.1 m/s and 2.4 m/s corresponding to the energy levels of 6 J and 8 J, respectively. The movement of the impactor was constrained in all the DOFs except U3 (displacement in the Z-axis). Four pins were used to securely fasten the composite specimen to prevent rebound impact throughout the test. An “Encastre” boundary condition was assigned to the reference point at the corner of the fixture plate to freeze it. The general contact algorithm was implemented for CFRP-impactor and CFRP-plate contact pairs. The properties of general contact were discretized by normal-hard and tangential penalty with a frictional index of 0.25. The cohesive contact model was applied to simulate debonding between the battery and CFRP, with specific properties shown in Table 5 .

To evaluate the applicability of the FEA model for the tensile test, the numerical results were compared with experimental data sourced from Pattarakunnan et al. [40]. The predicted stress–strain histories shown in Fig. 5 and the data in Table 6 exhibit a comparative correlation with the experimental results, especially on ultimate tensile strength and failure mechanisms. The specimens experienced stiffness degradation and ruptures at both loading ends and the area around the LiPo battery, as illustrated in Fig. 6 (a), which is consistent with the experimental observations [40]. However, the measured tensile modulus of the sandwich structure from the experiment, with and without batteries, is lower than the calculated modulus from the simulation. The primary reason for this discrepancy lies in the input elastic modulus of the main load-bearing component—CFRP. Since the modulus was sourced from literature and corresponding experimental tests, the manufacturing process may introduce deviations. Additionally, the insertion of the battery could cause unpredictable localized defects, leading to unquantifiable errors in the numerical simulations. Consequently, this resulted in an overestimation of the overall structural modulus of the sandwich composites. Despite this inconsistency in tensile modulus, the numerical model reliably reproduced the other key tensile parameters of sandwich composites and will be used to gather further information.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of integrating LiPo cells on the tensile properties of multifunctional sandwich composites
ce:section.ce:para: The obtained numerical data in Table 6 indicates that the placement of embedded LiPo batteries did not affect the modulus, ultimate strength, or failure strain of the sandwich composites with foam core when subjected to tensile loading. The strain and stress distribution in the CFRP face sheets remained uniform throughout, with no localized softening observed in the areas surrounding the batteries. These findings align well with the experimental results of Pattarakunnan et al. [40]. The consistent mechanical behaviour can be explained by the fact that the cut-offs for housing the batteries were only made on the foam core, which preserved the geometric integrity of the primary load-bearing component—the CFRP skin. In addition, the mechanical properties of the LiPo cell are comparable to the core, resulting in a low influence on the structural continuity and negligible impact on the overall performance of the sandwich composite.

However, increased Hashin damage indices were observed near the loading edge and on the adjacent side of the batteries, suggesting that the insertion of batteries would potentially change the breakdown location of the sandwich structure, as shown in Fig. 6 (a). Pattarakunnan et al. [40] stated that the fracture alternation may be ascribed to the cracking of the resin-rich region located between the battery and foam core. However, our numerical results suggest slight deviations from their proposal. The current FE model assumed a resin-free condition with all contact surfaces fully bonded, theoretically precluding interfacial cracking. Even so, fractures in close proximity to the battery edge were still found numerically. The underlying cause for this damage shift might be attributed to the stress concentration on the foam core, as depicted in Fig. 6 (b), leading to its weakening and reduced strength. This can be verified by examining Fig. 7 (a) and (b). For sandwich composite with three batteries, the specimen underwent breakdown at t = 5.2 ms. By further examining the damage distribution in the CFRP face sheet and foam core, it was observed that the damage coefficient of the foam adjacent to the battery reached its critical value before the CFRP face sheet in the same region, indicating that the foam core failed earlier than the CFRP skin. A similar phenomenon occurred in the sandwich composite with five batteries, as shown in Fig. 7 (b), where the foam core failed prior to the CFRP face sheet at t = 2.75 ms. Localized foam collapses can lead to a catastrophic rupture of the entire structure as the face sheets lose the necessary support [72]. In addition, Fig. 6 (a) illustrates tearing failure in the battery of the sandwich composite with one battery. It can be seen in Fig. 7 (c) that the ductile damage variables of the battery did not appear to approach the critical value before the rupture of the entire structure. The tearing occurred at t = 9 ms, after the stress–strain curve dropped at t = 5.3 ms. This suggests that the tearing failure of the battery seen in the numerical test was not due to the ultimate failure strength or strain limit of the battery itself but rather to the collapse of the entire sandwich composite. This structural collapse compromises the protective capability of the sandwich composite for battery, weakening the linkage between the foam core and the battery. Meanwhile, post-tensile testing observations reveal that the CFRP skin of the sandwich specimen with three batteries exhibited slight longitudinal slitting, whereas the other specimens broke cleanly, as shown in Fig. 6 (a) and also reported by Pereira et al. [20]. The preceding discourses demonstrate that battery insertion will alter how the structure fails during tension and the damage mechanism of each component within the sandwich structural batteries.

Fig. 8 demonstrates the correlation between the numerical outputs and the experimental curve from the literature [41]. The numerical model for composite laminates with embedded batteries shows an overestimation of the compressive modulus and an underestimation of the failure strain, with relative errors provided in Table 7 . This is consistent with the tensile case as discussed earlier. The nonlinearity in the experimental curve not captured by the computational model can be attributed to measurement instability rather than unpredictable plasticity, since the LiPo battery as the dominated plastic material did not reach its yielding point (4 % strain) and performed plastically until the entire structure failed (lower than 1 % strain). The compressive strength of composite laminate was defined as the point at which the Hashin damage variables of an entire cross-sectional CFRP surface reached one. The failure strength from numerical simulation remarkably aligns with the experimental result, as shown in Fig. 8 and Table 7. Meanwhile, the compression strain distributions of composite laminates with and without batteries shown in Fig. 9 (a) closely match the strain maps from Digital Image Correlation (DIC) in experimental testing. The physical experiment demonstrated that the fracture of the composite laminate containing batteries occurred at the CFRP-battery interface [41], which aligns with numerical model findings indicating structural stiffness degradation and material softening in this region, as illustrated in Fig. 10 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of integrating LiPo cells on the compressive properties of multifunctional composite laminates
ce:section.ce:para: Incorporating LiPo cells into composite laminate structures significantly lowers their compressive performances. Numerical results show a substantial decrease in both the compressive modulus (10 %∼25 %) and the compressive strength (49 %∼70 %) of the composite laminates, as detailed in Table 7. This contrasts with the tensile case since the material replaced by the softer battery in the current scenario was CFRP rather than PVC foam. The reduction in load-bearing capacity of the composite laminates was primarily attributed to disruptions in the internal fibre continuity caused by the cutoffs [41]. Furthermore, material substitution also resulted in stress and strain concentrations on the CFRP face sheets, as illustrated in Fig. 9 (a) and (b). These concentrations exacerbated the damage of the composite laminate, reducing the overall structural stiffness and strength. Stress concentration also altered the damage index map of the composite facings, with the Hashin coefficient at the battery locations consistently exceeding that of the surrounding laminate materials, as shown in Fig. 11 (a). Furthermore, for CFRP material, as loading progressed over time, the cell edge was the first area to reach its failure threshold, followed by the region overlapping the cell, and finally, the area around the cell. Though no battery failure was reported, the cells underwent plastic deformation during compression loading, as depicted in Fig. 11 (b). This deformation might cause debonding and internal buckling of internal layers due to the often-weak interlayer connections and consequent degradation of the electrical performance of the LiPo battery.

The structure featuring two horizontally oriented batteries exhibits lower strength and modulus than the laminate with the same number of vertically oriented batteries, as shown in Table 7. Moreover, the strength and modulus of the laminate embedded with one battery are comparable to those of the laminate with two vertical batteries. This suggests that the number of integrated batteries may not substantially affect the baseline composite laminate under compression; instead, the locations of cells critically influence compressive behaviour by reducing load-carrying cross-sectional areas [41]. Overall, the insertion of batteries changed the failure degradation of composite laminates and reduced their mechanical properties under compression. The significant weakening of strength and structural integrity raises the question of whether sacrificing mechanical performance for a slight increase in energy storage capacity is a worthwhile trade-off. To maintain the overall effectiveness and reliability of multifunctional energy storage systems, it is crucial to carefully balance mechanical robustness and energy storage capacity during the design phase.

The work conducted by Galos et al. [21] served as the reference for evaluating the capability of the numerical model for multifunctional energy storage systems subjected to three-point bending. The model-predicted loading histories and flexural strengths of sandwich beams under long-span bending strongly correlate with the experimental curves, as shown in Fig. 12 (a). Only a slight overestimation of the flexural modulus is observed, this is the same with tensile and compressive case. Additionally, the numerical simulation exhibited an earlier prediction of ruptures in the specimens with two batteries, showing a slight underestimation of failure displacements.

In short-span bending, the initial elastic region of each specimen is in perfect alignment with the experimental ones, as indicated in Fig. 12 (b). However, the model did not accurately capture the structural behaviour beyond the yield point, particularly for the sandwich composites containing one battery and two batteries 10 mm apart. This discrepancy might be attributed to the imprecise input of the plastic property of the foam core and LiPo cell. The foam played a crucial role in determining the behaviour of the sandwich composite under short-span bending since a plateau linked to the foam’s crushing was observed in all specimens. By reviewing the loading curve of specimen with one battery, the importance of the LiPo battery is also emphasized. Beyond the yielding point, instead of a direct decline, the loading curve for this specimen exhibited persistent large bending displacements, caused by the plastic hardening of the battery, which is consistent with the findings by Galos et al. [21], as shown in Fig. 12 (b). Although slight disparities exist between numerical and experimental curves, the overall predictability of the three-point bending model for the flexural strength and failure mechanism of sandwich structural batteries remains precise and reliable.

is the maximum applied load, L is the span length, b is the specimen width, and m is the slope of the initial elastic region of the load–displacement curve. t and c denote the thickness of the skin and core correspondingly.

The inclusion of batteries, regardless of their quantity and arrangement, did not have notable adverse effects on the flexural strength and modulus of the sandwich composites under both short- and long-span bending. As shown in Table 8 , multifunctional energy storage devices exhibited very slight reductions in flexural performance and an increase (∼11 %) in the ultimate failure stress was observed in specimens with one battery under long-span bending. A similar improvement was noted in sandwich composite with the same configuration during short-span bending, as discussed before. The enhancement results from substituting less durable foam beneath the loading point with a LiPo battery capable of enduring higher strain. Moreover, the plastic hinge formed on the battery surface prevented the continued advancement of structural collapse, as shown in Fig. 13 (a).

Failures in sandwich composites and their component materials are interrelated, with shear failure of the foam core initiating all subsequent damage in the sandwich composite. During short- and long-span bending, the foam core underwent shear cracking and indentation across all configurations. As shown in Fig. 14 , for the sandwich composite with two batteries spaced 40 mm apart, at t = 17.5 ms, the foam began to experience shear failure and crushing. At t = 20 ms, the initial tensile failure of the CFRP was detected, marked by a drop in the loading curve, indicating the onset of load-bearing capacity loss. The tensile failure of CFRP dominated initially, after t = 35 ms, compressive failure began to occur and became more significant. The loss in load-bearing capacity of the CFRP and the deterioration of structural integrity further accelerated the debonding of contact surfaces. Delamination failure was observed in the region below the loading point in all specimens, affecting CFRP-battery and CFRP-foam contact pairs, with the sandwich composite containing one battery under short-span bending exhibited the most severe delamination compared to the other specimens, as shown in Fig. 13 (b). This can be explained by the significant plastic deformation of the battery and the stress concentration caused by the battery. Hence, the interfacial failure in the multifunctional sandwich composite is a combined consequence of CFRP damage and the deformation of the inserted battery. Although the battery experienced plastically during loading, there were no indications of failure or removal of any elements. However, the electrical functionalization of the battery should be further validated considering such deformation.

The loading curves in Fig. 12 (b) show similar trends for the cases without battery and with two batteries positioned 10 mm and 40 mm apart, featuring a plateau followed by an initial load drop at the loading displacement of 4 mm and fluctuations in the loading curve between displacements of 4 and 8 mm. In contrast, for the sandwich composite containing one battery, the load did not instantly decline after yielding. Moreover, when the battery was positioned beneath the centre of the loading bar, foam shear cracking was less prominent compared to other specimens under short-span bending. This is because that the battery is the primary stressed component in this particular arrangement and its inclusion will lead to a modification in the post-yielding behaviour of the sandwich composite under short-span bending. These disparities were not seen in the long-span bending scenario, where the presence of batteries did not introduce significant alterations. In conclusion, the global mechanical response of the sandwich composite under long-span bending is mainly determined by the mechanical rigidity of the CFRP material from a macroscopic point of view. However, the mechanical response observed during short-span bending highly depends on the mechanical behaviour of specific material beneath the loading bar [21] and shows greater sensitivity to microscopic structural changes, indicating the importance of the cell’s location for optimal mechanical properties under bending loading.

Overall, the embedded battery had no negative effect on the sandwich composite during long-span bending, whether in terms of the flexural properties or damage modes. Nevertheless, it altered the post-yielding behaviour of structures under short-span bending. Modelling the mechanical behaviour of sandwich composite with foam core and batteries during short-span bending is a challenging task, as it requires a precise material model for batteries and foam. Even slight variations in a single parameter could lead to substantial deviations in the loading curve and flexural properties. Ensuring the high fidelity of the model necessitates systematic experimental measurements for every component of the cell and interlayer interaction coefficient. In addition, an in-depth understanding of the foam’s crushable behaviour is essential, especially in the crushable parameters under hydrostatic loading, which might be valuable for future efforts in developing the modelling of multifunctional energy storage systems.

The amount of energy absorbed by composite laminate under low-velocity impact was chosen to validate the FEA model with the experimental data [57]. The incident energy absorption of the material can be calculated through

The predicted absorption efficiencies closely align with the experimental results for specimens with and without batteries, as depicted in Fig. 15 (b). The numerical model can accurately reproduce the damage that occurred within the composite structure embedded with the battery during the impact test, including indentation, CFRP face-sheet cracking, and plastic deformation of the cell, as shown in Fig. 16 (a). This correlation was demonstrated by comparing the numerical results with the X-ray CT cross-sectional images obtained from physical tests [57]. The calculated indentation depth and damage area of composite laminate shown in Table 9 also exhibit strong alignment with the experimental results, confirming the practicality of the FEA model for further analysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of integrating LiPo cells on the low-velocity impact responses of multifunctional composite laminates
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 15 (a) presents the impact energy histories of composite structures obtained from FE simulations. Incorporating LiPo cells significantly increased the energy absorption efficiency of the composite laminate, absorbing 7.8 times more energy during a 6 J impact and 4.67 times more energy after an 8 J impact. The excessive energy absorption is due to the rupture of CFRP face sheets, permanent deformation of the battery, damage to the cell itself, and delamination at the interface surfaces [57]. A variation in the slope of the kinetic energy curve for composite laminate embedded with battery is observed around t = 2–2.5 ms for both 6 J and 8 J impact, marking the moment when the battery and CFRP reached their damage point. This indicates that while the inclusion of the battery increased the energy absorption capability of the composite laminate, the CFRP material provided minimal protection for the battery in this configuration, causing the battery to start degrading at the very beginning of impact loading. The severe damage to the battery can be attributed to the stress concentration in the composite laminate surrounding the cell, as seen in Fig. 16 (b). Internal fractures within the battery can lead to short circuits and a reduced capacity to store energy. Hence, it is crucial to give extra focus to safety factors when designing multifunctional composite laminates subjected to impact loading scenario.

Fig. 16 (a) shows that the 6 J and 8 J impacts did not damage the composite laminates without batteries. After the projectile rebound, no remaining stress field was visible on the top and bottom CFRP surface. The FE model only identified minor element deletion on the lower surface of composite laminate. In contrast, multifunctional energy storage systems with battery showed heavy cracking and penetrating damage on both the top and bottom surfaces under 6 J and 8 J impact, as seen in Fig. 16 (c). The failure of CFRP can be linked to replacing the stiffer and brittle CFRP with a weaker and softer battery, and the substitution decreases the total load-bearing ability of the composite laminate [57]. Meanwhile, in the case of composite laminate with battery, the battery’s exposure was evident at the lower part when subjected to 8 J impact, which resulted from the cracking on the bottom surface of CFRP. The exposure within composite laminate under high-energy impact will raise the possibility of electrolyte leakage, leading to an explosion and posing a safety issue. Fig. 16 (d) shows the damage area to the upper surface of CFRP due to the impact. The 8 J impact test revealed a greater extent of damage compared to the 6 J impact test, suggesting that the specimen received a higher amount of destructive energy. Even though no obvious separation of the contact surface between the battery and CFRP is seen in Fig. 16 (a), an increased cohesive damage parameter has been detected at the interface between the lower CFRP skin and the battery cell around the loading point for the specimen exposed to 8 J impact. This implies a potential debonding failure occurred during low-velocity impact, as illustrated in Fig. 16 (e).

In summary, while including LiPo cells enhanced energy absorption efficiency, it also introduced significant challenges in terms of structural integrity and safety under impact loadings. The increased energy absorption is beneficial, but the associated damage and potential safety risks necessitate careful consideration in the design and implementation of multifunctional composite laminates under impact.

This paper presents an investigation into the numerical methodology for analysing multifunctional composites that contain inserted LiPo batteries under various loading conditions, including tension, compression, three-point bending, and low-velocity impact. The model employed a bottom-up approach, integrating detailed physical equations, plasticity, and damage mechanisms from the material level to depict the structural-level mechanical characteristics of structural batteries. The main conclusions drawn from this study are as follows,
•
The numerical results exhibit considerable accuracy compared to experimental data. The FE model effectively replicates the real failure mechanisms of composite structural batteries and explores how integrated elements affect the original performance of the host structure, confirming the physical interpretability of the numerical models under various external loads for such complicated structures. However, it still shows limitations in reproducing the loading modulus and the post-yielding behaviour of sandwich structural batteries when subjected to short-span bending.
•
The sandwich structural batteries proved to be safe under tension and three-point bending. The external CFRP face sheets provided adequate safeguarding for the internal batteries until the entire specimen ruptured, and the LiPo batteries did not compromise the stiffness and strength of the original structure.
•
Significant reductions in the mechanical properties of composite laminates were observed under compression and low-velocity impact due to structural discontinuities introduced by the cells. Especially in the case of low-velocity impact, the composite laminate failed to provide sufficient protection for the battery from the very beginning.
•
The damage modelling provides a deeper understanding of the damage degradation of structural batteries and their individual components. The inclusion of batteries altered the structural behaviour and failure mechanisms of the multifunctional structural batteries across all loading scenarios. These changes can be attributed to stress concentrations, geometrical discontinuities from batteries, interfacial delamination, and material substitutions at critical loading sites.

Overall, the numerical methodology employed in this study provides valuable insights that may not be captured through experimental tests, offering useful suggestions for the safety design and optimization of multifunctional energy storage systems. Future developments could extend this work to more complex loading conditions, such as fatigue, high-velocity impact, or post-impact compression, to better understand the long-term performance of multifunctional structures and replicate practical applications. Additionally, although the cell did not fail mechanically in most of the discussed cases, electrochemical degradation due to internal interlayer delamination caused by large deformations, as well as thermal effects, require further research into multiphysics coupling analysis. This would offer a more detailed evaluation of the diverse functions of structural batteries. To enhance practical applicability, future studies could also integrate structural health monitoring strategies for real-time damage assessment, such as piezoelectric sensors, strain gauges, or AI-driven detection systems. Additionally, considering manufacturing feasibility, exploration of alternative materials will be needed for advancing these multifunctional systems.
